content.full_text:
State of California 
Memor 
d 
To 
: Staff involved in providing advice 
Dote 
: May 21, 1980 
From 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Jay 
Subject: Section 84214 
The following questions regarding late contributions were received 
from John Hodgson: 
1. 
Can the recipient of late contributions identify 
multiple and different sources on a single telegram? 
2. 
Can the donor of late contributions identify mUltiple 
and different recipients on a single telegram? 
3. 
Can multiple recipients, for which Hodgson is the 
treasurer/agent, be identified on a single telegram 
when they file their late contribution report? 
4. 
Can multiple donors, for which Hodgson is the 
treasurer/agent, be identified on a single telegram 
when they file their late contribution report? 
5. 
When Hodgson is the treasurer/agent for both, can 
the donor and the donor's recipient (or the recipient 
and the recipient's donor) file a single late contri-
bution report? 
My advice was "yes" to questions 1 and 2 and "no" to questions 3, 
4 and 5. 
John Hodgson was not overly enthusiastic with my last 
three answers, particularly the answer to question 5. 


Thomas M. Sobel, Esq. 
Chief, Legal Division 
DOBBS & NIELSEN 
<;;7/tto'l'neIP and ~Wl3eu,7L4 at SCzw 
SUITE 550,1225 EIGHTH STREET 
SACRAMENTO, CA 95814 
(916) 446-6752 
March 10, 1980 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, California 
95814 
Re: 
Responsibilities of Campaign Treasurers 
Dear Mr. Sobel: 
Thank you for your letter of December 14, 1979 in 
reply to my correspondence of October 25, 1979. 
The purpose of 
my letter was to seek better clarification of the duties of a 
committee treasurer. 
The motivation for this request is our 
concern that Regulation 18427, including the Comment, does not 
seem to provide treasurers with clear and adequate notice of 
their responsibilities, particularly in light of the recent 
FPPC lawsuit against a number of committees (and their campaign 
consultant Butcher/Ford) and also, the recent FPPC enforcement 
matter against the Quentin Kopp Committee. 
In the lawsuit, the 
FPPC informs that court that treasurers should know how to 
properly disclose expenditures containing commissions and 
public relations fees, even though Regulation 18427 focuses 
almost totally on contributions and your Campaign Disclosure 
Manual is virtually silent on this subject. 
In the Kopp case, 
the FPPC staff is questioning the diligence of a treasurer even 
though the treasurer did question the source of the contribution, 
did ask the candidate to obtain a transmittal letter from the 
supposed donor to confirm his identity, and did receive the 
confirming letter. 
The question raised is how far past the invoice must 
the treasurer look before paying a bill and what further itemiza-
tion is required on the campaign report other than the payee. 
For example, 1) must a treasurer disclose only "pass through" 
payments or commissions to people involved in the vendor's 
trade (travel agency, etc.) when such commissions have no 
benefit to any political persons? 
2) Must a treasurer disclose 
subcontracting done by a vendor on the vendor's own initiation 
in the regular course of the vendor's business if such subcon-
tracting payments by the vendor are not itemized on the invoice 
(i.e., a printer who subcontracts any or all of the design, 
SAN FRANCISCO OFFICE· SUITE 2500. THE ALCOA 8LOG .. ONE MARITIME PLAZA. SAN FRANCISCO. CA 94111 • (415) 362-1940 
LOS ANGELES OFFICE· SUITE 309. 612 SOUTH FLOWER STREET. LOS ANGELES. CA 90017· (213) 628-5867 


Thomas M. Sobel, Esq. 
Re: 
Responsiblities of Campaign Treasurers 
March 10, 1980 
Page Two 
layout, printing, delivering, etc. of a printing job)? 3) Is 
disclosure required of any nonpolitical personnel specifically 
hired by a vendor to perform work on the committee's job (i.e., 
a printer hires an extra assistant for two days to assist the 
printing job or a CPA hires an additional bookkeeper during an 
election year to help on various campaign accounts)? There are 
a number of other variations of these situations which are 
raised by Government Code §842l0(h) and §84303. 
It is clear that the FPPC believes that certain 
payments by the payees are disclosable (payments by advertising 
firms to the media, etc.), but the FPPC has given virtually no 
clarification of its view on what other component payments are 
disclosable. 
Further, the Comments to Regulation 18427 are primarily 
oriented to the treasurer's responsibility to question "suspect" 
contribution data. 
What requirements are imposed on the treasurer 
as it relates to expenditures? 
Does a treasurer need to require 
an affidavit by all vendors to accompany each invoice stating 
that the vendor understand the requirements of Government Code 
§842l0(h) and §84303 and has not made any such payments as part 
of the invoice being submitted? 
As mentioned in my October 25, 1979 letter, our 
office would be please to work with you and your staff to 
further explain the current confusions, as well as to work 
toward a clarifying regulation or opinion. 
Very sincerely, 
~~~z 
~HODGSON II 
JHH:laj

sections.question_synthetic: The document contains questions regarding campaign treasurer responsibilities, specifically: 1) Whether multiple sources/recipients can be identified on a single telegram for late contributions, and 2) Whether donors and recipients can file joint late contribution reports when the same person serves as treasurer/agent for both.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC Chief Counsel Thomas M. Sobel provided advice that multiple sources and recipients can be identified on single telegrams (questions 1-2: yes), but multiple recipients/donors cannot be identified on a single telegram or in a joint report when the same treasurer/agent is involved (questions 3-5: no).
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains mixed content: an internal memo regarding late contributions advice and a separate incoming letter about treasurer responsibilities. The memo shows informal Q&A format without formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections. The second letter discusses broader treasurer duties but is not formatted as a typical advice letter response. Confidence reduced due to unclear document boundaries and mixed document types.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The document contains questions regarding campaign treasurer responsibilities, specifically: 1) Whether multiple sources/recipients can be identified on a single telegram for late contributions, and 2) Whether donors and recipients can file joint late contribution reports when the same person serves as treasurer/agent for both.

CONCLUSION: FPPC Chief Counsel Thomas M. Sobel provided advice that multiple sources and recipients can be identified on single telegrams (questions 1-2: yes), but multiple recipients/donors cannot be identified on a single telegram or in a joint report when the same treasurer/agent is involved (questions 3-5: no).

embedding.summary: This document is an internal FPPC memorandum from May 21, 1980 summarizing advice given to John Hodgson regarding late contribution reporting requirements under Section 84214, along with a March 10, 1980 letter from Hodgson seeking clarification on campaign treasurer disclosure duties regarding expenditures and subcontractor payments.
